title: TBM 273: What To Do More (And Less) Of
questions: What observable behaviors indicate a team is becoming more product-centric?
How can we create an environment where these behaviors emerge more often?
What should we do less of to prevent inertia, premature convergence, and output-only thinking?
How should leaders balance evidence gathering with conviction and experimentation?
summary: The post outlines a set of observable behaviors that characterize product-centric teams, aimed at leaders and product operations/product community audiences who want practical signals of “how good looks.” It focuses on decision hygiene (context, delayed convergence, crisp framing), learning loops (direct customer contact, adaptive research), and organizational dynamics (bidirectional strategy, questioning priors, leverage seeking). It also frames these behaviors as prompts for reflection—what to do more of and what to do less of—rather than all-or-nothing rules.

title: A Cagan Critique
questions: Does a critique of product leadership unfairly turn empathy into a moral judgment about ambition?
How do we distinguish contextual awareness from resignation when adapting practices across markets and cultures?
What makes operating models transferable, and what is lost when context is ignored?
How should product leaders balance systems constraints with individual agency to drive improvement?
summary: This post is a respectful counterpoint to a prominent product leadership argument, aimed at product managers and product leaders who want to think critically about how “best practices” are framed and applied. It challenges a rhetorical pattern that moves from empathy to moral judgment, and argues for systems thinking: transfer operating models only when underlying context and constraints are understood. It also contrasts steady continuous improvement with the churn and performative pivots common in tech organizations, advocating curiosity and multiple valid paths to excellence.

title: TBM 320: From Fluffy Concepts to Concrete Outcomes & Behaviors
questions: What observable behaviors would we see more or less of if we were truly more empowered or more data-driven?
Why do people advocate for or resist practices they haven’t tried first-hand?
How do we move from desired outcomes to specific behaviors and measurable conditions for change?
What interventions are most likely to enable behavior change given capability, opportunity, and motivation constraints?
search_terms: Talk to the Elephant behavior change
summary: This post is for product leaders and product teams who are frustrated that aspirational concepts like empowerment and being data-driven don’t translate into day-to-day practice. It argues for replacing vague goals with observable behaviors, then using COM-B and behavior change techniques to diagnose barriers and choose interventions. The guidance is especially relevant when teams need to improve how they review metrics and make decisions.

title: Keep Product Management Messy
questions: Why does product management look different across companies and teams?
How should I interpret product advice when my context doesn’t match the examples?
What learning approach works best for mastering product management in a context-dependent role?
Does the lack of a single definition mean product management is flawed?
search_terms: why there is no silver bullet in product management
summary: The post argues that product management varies widely because it is influenced by many company and market dimensions, making the role hard to define consistently. It’s aimed at product leaders and aspiring PMs who are frustrated by generic advice and want a better way to learn and apply guidance in context. The core takeaway is to treat complexity as expected and shift from one-size-fits-all teaching toward guided, hands-on apprenticeship and sense-making.

title: TBM 317: Yes, Or...
questions: Which popular product and leadership principles have hidden failure modes?
How do you balance clear objectives with avoiding tunnel vision?
What should you measure when not everything that matters is measurable?
How do you manage stakeholder pressure on roadmaps without shipping dud features?
search_terms: sunk cost fallacy in product decisions
summary: This post is a collection of product management and leadership “maxims” that argues each well-known principle can produce distortions when applied without context. It’s for product leaders and managers who want sharper decision-making around objectives, measurement, feedback, transparency, hiring, and roadmap governance.

title: TBM 311: Roadmapping Miro Board and Question for Subscribers
questions: Should a product/engineering newsletter be split into separate tracks for actionable tactics versus existential/organizational topics?
How do you prevent content recommendations from feeling like 'word salad' to readers who want practical guidance?
What is the best way to write for a highly diverse product development audience with different mental models and interests?
search_terms: organizational design and politics at work
how to write for diverse audiences
summary: The post shares a Miro board resource for facilitating team roadmapping discussions and asks readers for input on newsletter direction. It argues that mixing tactical, actionable posts with dense existential/mental-model content can polarize readers in a diverse product development audience. The author considers splitting the newsletter into two tracks—one for tools and step-by-step advice and one for macro-level organizational and individual challenges.

title: Product Coaching with Petra Wille
questions: When is coaching the wrong intervention and training or self-learning the better choice?
How do product leaders coach across hierarchy levels without overstepping boundaries of influence?
What practices build a culture of informal peer coaching and mentoring inside organizations?
How can leaders regulate emotions and handle difficult conversations without suppressing feelings?
summary: This post is an interview focused on how to use coaching effectively in product leadership contexts, including when coaching is a bad idea and how product coaching differs from executive coaching. It’s aimed at product leaders and managers who want practical guidance on building coaching cultures through communities of practice, improving listening and peer support, and handling emotional energy and difficult conversations. The discussion also emphasizes boundaries of influence, managing up, and creating time for thinking to enable directional clarity.

title: TBM 289: The Boring Product Manifesto
questions: Why does product culture feel so dramatic and high-stakes right now?
How can teams separate corporate machinations and macro noise from the core work of building products?
What does “back to basics” really mean without becoming gaslighting?
How do psychological safety and focus enable better product execution?
summary: The post argues that product work is often best understood as a steady, competence-driven job rather than a constant drama-filled transformation narrative. It’s aimed at product leaders and builders who feel overwhelmed by industry angst and want a practical mindset for regaining tempo, focus, and psychological safety while shipping useful products.

title: TBM 279: How to Learn and Practice Product Management in a Feature Factory
questions: How can I practice product management when my company is a feature factory?
What should I measure to demonstrate impact if no one cares about metrics?
How do I build customer discovery habits in an org where PMs don’t do it?
How can I learn the business model and KPIs to connect product work to outcomes?
search_terms: how to practice PM in a feature factory
summary: This post offers practical, low-risk ways for aspiring or practicing product managers to build real PM skills inside organizations that behave like feature factories. It focuses on getting reps through instrumentation and metrics, customer and stakeholder discovery, learning business KPIs, and collaborating with designers and engineers. The guidance is aimed at PMs who want to improve their craft despite constraints and who may be preparing for internal change or future job opportunities.

title: TBM 278: The Power of Firsthand Experience
questions: What product experiences help people truly understand product thinking?
How do you change stakeholder beliefs when they only know product through proxies?
Why does firsthand customer and delivery experience improve decision quality and velocity?
What should a team do differently if they haven’t seen discovery and iteration succeed?
summary: The post argues that people in product organizations often misjudge product practices because they haven’t seen the underlying realities firsthand. It offers a set of concrete experiences—ranging from flat launches and pivots to PMF, organic adoption, feature-factory decay, and direct customer access—that help stakeholders internalize product thinking. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who need to align others by giving them real exposure, including to failures and false starts.

title: TBM 272: The Biggest Opportunity (Part 2)
questions: How can we get more value out of skilled pragmatists without pressuring them into misaligned behaviors?
What specific behaviors should we ask for (or stop asking for) in product development teams?
Why do people in the same role behave differently across teams and organizational contexts?
How do we design interventions that account for both individual motivations and structural incentives?
search_terms: risk vs reward norms in organizations
behavior change framework who what extent context outcome
listening to employees to change culture
summary: This post is for product and engineering leaders who want to improve outcomes by changing how teams behave in product development, especially when “skilled pragmatists” don’t naturally go above and beyond. It argues that vague exhortations aren’t enough: leaders should define the target behavior precisely and then use bias-aware, qualitative listening across different employee mindsets to understand what enables or blocks the behavior. The goal is to move from “getting more out of people” to co-designing interventions that help teams achieve more together.

title: TBM 250: Dear Executive...
questions: Why does my product-development message not land with executives?
How do experts think about tradeoffs differently from beginners?
What’s the difference between a tradeoff and a polarity in product decisions?
How can teams find common ground when each side has different mental models?
search_terms: context switching costs in product teams
how to explain product development to executives
summary: This post is a communication and alignment guide for product practitioners trying to explain modern product development to executives who may find the message confusing or un-actionable. It uses the shift from beginner to expert mental models—tradeoffs, polarities, and “liminal spaces”—to argue that misalignment often comes from different ways of perceiving constraints and limits. It’s aimed at helping readers reframe conversations to build shared understanding and improve decision-making.

title: TBM 233: Coffee & Complexity
questions: How do you communicate complex product/process knowledge to get buy-in in an hour?
Why do playbooks and simplified frameworks break down for “The Thing” work?
What’s the best way to capture and share tribal knowledge across diverse teams?
When should you use external consultants versus internal templates and onboarding materials?
summary: The post explores why complex product and organizational know-how is hard to package into playbooks, principles, or simplified slides, arguing that the actionable knowledge is embedded in the “it depends” questions. It’s aimed at product and organizational leaders who need ways to share tribal knowledge, onboard new people, and secure buy-in while recognizing that organizations are messy, satisficing, and meaning-generating systems.

title: TBM 211: Product-Reality Fit (PRF)
questions: How do you maintain product reality fit after you already have product-market fit?
Why do companies lose alignment between product and reality during scaling and diversification?
What practices help prevent overconfidence and inertia from derailing new initiatives?
How should companies balance core customer focus with learning about new customer segments?
search_terms: resource allocation bandwidth for product teams
summary: The post explains why product-market fit is only the beginning and how companies can still fail by losing product reality fit when they pursue new initiatives. It’s aimed at product leaders and founders who are scaling and need practical ways to avoid overconfidence, competency drift, and validation gaps while reallocating resources and staying customer-aware.

title: TBM 8/52: Top 1% Product Managers
questions: Why is product management so obsessed with “top 1%” and other bromide titles?
Are these narratives driven by content incentives, role ambiguity, or something about the people who become PMs?
Do “top percentile” stories overstate how much PM skill translates into outcomes?
How can PM content better support diversity and reduce insecurity-driven mythology?
search_terms: how to talk about competence in product roles
diversity in product management hiring and storytelling
summary: This post critiques the recurring “top 1%” and “great product managers” trope in product management content, arguing that it’s fueled by clickbait incentives, role ambiguity, and Silicon Valley meritocratic mythology. It’s aimed at PMs and aspiring PMs who want a more grounded way to discuss competence, impact, and career development—especially in service of greater diversity.

title: TBM 4/52: "Sense, Agency, and Hustle"
questions: Are terms like product sense, empathy, and high agency describing skills, traits, or competencies—and can they be learned?
How does company culture change the meaning of “ownership,” “directness,” or “no excuses” in job descriptions and career ladders?
When does defining product effectiveness improve clarity versus when does it become accidental gatekeeping?
How can leaders describe what and why (outcomes) without prescribing a culturally specific how (style and personality)?
summary: The post argues that common product management descriptors (e.g., empathy, agency, hustle, ownership) are often culturally loaded and therefore ambiguous, which can confuse newcomers and unintentionally gatekeep the profession. It focuses on how “product sense” and related ideas lack consensus because they blend skills, traits, and competencies, and because job language reflects underlying worldviews. The intended audience includes product leaders, educators, and hiring managers who want more inclusive, context-aware ways to define and teach product effectiveness.

title: TBM in 2023
questions: How can product advice be actionable without becoming context-free or misleading?
What should a creator do when their audience’s needs shift from mess to solutions?
How do we increase diversity in product management guidance so it fits more worldviews?
What community formats help neurodivergent or non-mainstream tech practitioners feel less marginalized?
summary: The post outlines a creator’s priorities for product-management content in 2023, focusing on making advice more actionable while acknowledging the risks of context-free guidance. It also argues that mainstream product advice is insufficiently diverse and that a systems-thinking, neurodivergent-leaning audience needs community and peer support rather than feeling marginalized.

title: High Leverage Product Evangelism
questions: How do we build an evangelism program for a SaaS product?
What expertise should we map internally to support advocacy, education, and content?
How should we structure evangelism across community, content, and education?
Should we hire one superstar evangelist or build an ecosystem of complementary contributors?
summary: This post provides a practical framework for building high-leverage product evangelism in SaaS by mapping internal and external expertise across multiple levels and domains. It argues that evangelism/advocacy/thought leadership works best as an integrated system—community, content, and education/helping/coaching—coordinated around the right audiences and knowledge gaps. It is aimed at product marketing, growth, and enablement leaders who want to operationalize evangelism without relying on a single “superstar” hire.

title: TBM 44/52: Making Optional Product Practices Stick
questions: Why do teams struggle to make proactive, optional product practices stick even when they agree they’re valuable?
How can we diagnose the real blockers to learning and applying a new practice beyond just providing knowledge?
What concrete tactics help teams form habits like continuously updated information radiators?
How should we size and timebox experiments so they don’t fade after a good retrospective?
search_terms: learning gaps knowledge skills motivation habit environment
why good ideas fade quickly retrospective
summary: This post is about why teams struggle to sustain proactive, optional product practices (like continuous information radiators) and how to make them stick. It uses Julie Dirksen’s five learning gaps to diagnose blockers beyond knowledge, then offers practical tactics for habit formation, environment design, and experiment sizing. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to improve operating rhythms without falling into short-termism.

title: TBM 43/52: Clarity & Inspiration
questions: Does the need to inspire engineers bias product managers toward certainty theater?
What tactics help inspire engineering when uncertainty is high?
How much inspiration is enough, and can teams be too inspired?
If engineering can push back but doesn't, what does that indicate about strategy clarity and organizational dynamics?
search_terms: single owner clarity vs shared ownership
summary: The post explores how product strategy clarity is shaped by the need to inspire engineering, especially under uncertainty. It uses the drivers/constraints/floats framing as a backdrop and raises questions about whether inspiration can lead to certainty theater, when clarity is demanded by forcing functions, and what engineering pushback reveals about organizational design. It is aimed at product leaders and teams thinking about how to align strategy and execution.

title: TBM 11/52: From Research to Outcomes
questions: Where is the current bottleneck in our company’s research-to-outcomes flow, and does it move over time?
Are we choosing the right research goals and matching methods to those goals with sufficient rigor?
Why does our research fail to influence decisions and downstream actions?
Are we embedding the right people in research, and are we balancing rigor with the pace of development?
summary: The post proposes a high-level model for how research becomes outcomes, emphasizing the chain from research goals and method selection through inclusion, rigor, decision influence, and action/outcome quality. It is aimed at product and UX research teams who struggle with unproductive debates about qual vs quant, upfront vs continuous, and democratized research, and want a shared framework to diagnose where the pipeline breaks. It also highlights common failure modes such as wrong goals, method mismatch, research happening in a vacuum, rushed continuous discovery, and inability to act due to constraints like tech debt.

title: TBM 53/52: The Messifesto
questions: Why do product teams and leaders give such conflicting advice about what works?
How can we recognize and work through our own explanation biases in product leadership?
What practices help teams learn in complex, context-dependent environments?
How do psychological safety and culture affect product discovery and performance?
summary: The post argues that product leadership and organizational improvement are inherently biased by practitioners’ experience profiles and worldview, making many “universal” claims misleading. It’s aimed at product leaders and team members who want a more humble, context-aware approach to diagnosing what’s working, running safer experiments, and inviting diverse perspectives to navigate complexity.

title: TBM 52A/52: Should PdMs "Write Tickets" ?
questions: Should product managers write tickets/specs, or should engineers and the team handle that work?
What organizational conditions reduce the need for rigid stories and specifications?
How do incentives and measurement schemes turn product artifacts into transactional commitments?
How can teams shift from 'build the right thing' vs 'build it right' toward shared outcome ownership?
summary: This post argues that the question of whether product managers should write tickets/specs is less about doctrine and more about the team’s operating conditions and incentives. It’s aimed at product leaders and agile practitioners who want to reduce premature convergence and transactional “commitment” artifacts by increasing shared context, engineering empowerment, direct customer access, and outcome ownership.

title: TBM 50/52: Fragile Models
questions: How do we prevent research from rotting when we turn it into a reusable model or framework?
What risks arise when people use a model without understanding its provenance, caveats, and intended scope?
How can we design models so they remain editable, challengeable, and connected to the underlying evidence?
When is a simplified “one message for everyone” approach likely to fail, especially at the edges?
search_terms: making the scope of a model explicit
George Box all models are wrong but useful
summary: The post argues that turning messy research into simplified models (e.g., personas and messaging frameworks) can create “fragile” artifacts that lose context, provenance, and update pathways. It’s aimed at researchers, product/marketing teams, and designers who operationalize insights into reusable frameworks and want to reduce the risk of overconfidence, misuse, and harmful mono-process outcomes.

title: TBM 25/52: Self-Serving Bias, Advice from Silicon Valley, and Context
questions: What about our context are we taking for granted when we give or receive product advice?
Which prerequisites are missing when we copy Silicon Valley practices into a different environment?
Are we discounting advice because we assume our context is uniquely different, or because we’re using context as a scapegoat?
How can we sense our context well enough to adapt practices instead of assuming they generalize?
search_terms: overconfidence and stagnation in product management
summary: The post argues that product advice and organizational success are heavily context-dependent, and that people systematically misattribute outcomes through self-serving bias. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who consume or dispense “best practices” (especially from Silicon Valley) and need a way to check prerequisites, avoid overconfidence, and adapt to their own operating conditions.

title: TBM 21/52: Building Stuff to Close Deals
questions: How can I push back on sales requests to build features just to close deals?
What makes a product strategy compelling enough to align the whole organization?
Why do cost-and-complexity arguments fail to change minds in sales-driven environments?
How can we build an impact narrative with practical measurement when we can't be 100% certain?
search_terms: push back on sales feature requests
summary: This post helps product and engineering leaders respond to frequent sales pressure to build features purely to close deals. It argues that cost/rationality arguments usually fail, and that persuasion requires a believable product strategy, an impact narrative backed by observable track record, and a degree of faith while measurement uncertainty is reduced over time. It’s aimed at teams trying to maintain product focus in HIPPO- and sales-driven feature-factory environments.

title: TBM 18/52: Product Manager to People Manager (and My Big Mistake)
questions: What does clarity look and feel like for each person on my team?
How do I align on strategy and plans when different people define them differently?
What should I do when cross-functional clarity needs conflict?
How can a former product manager unlearn informal influence and build effective people management habits?
search_terms: product manager to people manager transition
how to ask what clarity looks like
unlearning product management habits for leadership
summary: The post is a practical reflection for experienced individual contributors—especially product managers—who are transitioning into people management after a long hiatus. It argues that alignment fails when leaders assume shared definitions of “clarity” and instead recommends repeatedly asking individuals what clarity looks and feels like, then digging into the underlying human needs. It also highlights how cross-functional teams can have opposing clarity requirements and why formal authority and decisive action become essential.

title: TBM 10/52: Flow of Novel Moments
questions: How can we support a steady stream of celebratory novelty moments during product development?
If we’re in a rut, what practical changes help break the cycle?
How do we tell whether we’re truly learning and integrating versus just being productive?
What role does beneficial friction and smaller iterations play in creating “corner-turning” moments?
search_terms: customer use in a new way
how to break a rut in product development
summary: This post argues that product teams should look for “novel moments” (sparks of insight, feedback, and customer discovery) rather than only measuring progress or productivity. It’s for product leaders and teams who want a systems-thinking way to interpret emotional signals as evidence of learning and integration, and to adjust their approach when those sparks are missing.

title: TBM 9/52: Coherence & Uncertainty
questions: How can teams make progress when they must proceed with incomplete information?
What causes resentment when one team member treats an MVP as an experiment and others treat it as a commitment?
How do we align on what 'good enough' means during product development?
What practices help teams manage uncertainty without pretending everything is fine?
summary: The post argues that product teams often advance by introducing constraints, accepting incomplete information, and temporarily suspending critique—but they do so without shared intent, causing misalignment and trust loss. It offers practical discipline for handling uncertainty: use clear language, catalog cut corners, log decisions and assumptions, and schedule revisits so experiments and questions are treated as such. This is aimed at product managers and cross-functional teams trying to improve coherence while moving fast under uncertainty.

title: TBM 47/53: Thinking Like a Designer/Product Manager
questions: Why do product teams fall into traps like assuming they can impersonate customers or skipping real customer research?
What is the true job of a maturity model for the humans using it (sales, marketing, customers, prospects)?
How should teams design maturity models as systems that connect research, assessment, communication, and interventions?
How do people learn to dig deeper into product problems beyond the obvious interface?
search_terms: thinking like a designer product manager
customer research is a learned skill
summary: The post argues that product managers and designers should treat “simple” truths—especially talking to real customers—as learned capabilities rather than assumptions. Using B2B maturity models as an example, it reframes them as multi-user, multi-component products whose real job is to create alignment, guidance, and improvement decisions. It’s aimed at product builders who want to avoid common design traps and go deeper than the standard linear 1–5 slide.

title: TBM 44/53: What Is Your Team Stubborn About?
questions: What rituals does your team keep doing even when they feel inefficient or irrational?
Which practices are you stubborn about in both words and actions?
How do you design onboarding so product managers connect with customers and other departments?
What long-term habits does your team sustain that others might call boring or unnecessary?
summary: The post argues that strong teams maintain specific rituals and standards—sometimes seemingly inefficient ones—because they grant themselves permission to care unequivocally about them. It uses examples like a 60-day onboarding rotation for product managers and practices such as blameless post-mortems and consistent usability testing, aimed at leaders and product organizations trying to build durable culture and customer connection.

title: TBM 27/53: The Lure of New Features and Products
questions: Why do teams keep shipping new features instead of fixing underlying product problems?
How do incentives and optics shape what makes it onto the roadmap?
What happens when core adoption, customer outcome, or value proposition issues are not addressed?
How can leaders shift incentives toward optimizing existing product areas?
search_terms: least resistance path in product strategy
fixing underlying product issues vs shipping features
summary: This post examines why B2B teams often default to a “feature factory” approach that prioritizes shipping new, marketable capabilities over messy optimization work. It argues that the behavior is driven by incentives and optics—visible wins are easier to achieve—leading to roadmap gaps around adoption, customer outcomes, and value proposition problems. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to realign strategy and execution toward harder, underlying fixes.

title: TBM 21/53: "Vision" and Prescriptive Roadmaps
questions: How do you communicate product vision so people trust it and feel commitment?
Why do feature factories persist even when teams do good work?
What does it mean to sell outcomes instead of selling future features?
How can an outcome-oriented roadmap avoid getting reset by leadership overcorrection?
summary: The post argues that “product vision” often functions as a trust and commitment signal, so many organizations respond better to prescriptive roadmaps that clearly state what will be built. It explains how incentives and organizational optics can entrench feature-factory behavior, and why outcome-focused teams may still struggle without actively selling outcomes and broadcasting real results. It’s aimed at product leaders and product managers trying to shift from feature-centric roadmaps to outcome-oriented planning without losing organizational buy-in.

title: TBM 20/52: Questions and Context
questions: What must we eventually know to make good decisions at the right cadence?
Why are experts asking so many questions—what decision are those questions meant to inform?
How do professional culture clashes cause mistrust or confusion during product development?
How can teams communicate product strategy context so architects and designers can align?
search_terms: asking better questions for product strategy
summary: The post explores how expert questioning can be misread as mistrust during product development, especially when professionals come from different backgrounds. It uses a chef and an architect example to argue that questions often exist to gather the context needed for hard decisions, and that communication improves when teams clarify what the question is for and what must be known by a certain decision cadence. It’s aimed at product, design, and engineering leaders who want to reduce friction and align around product strategy.

title: TBM 16/53: The Boring Bits
questions: How can a product team get buy-in for the unglamorous process work that enables delivery?
What should teams do when learning reviews or learning backlogs feel awkward or inefficient at first?
How do you design and run process experiments without overwhelming the team?
How do you build trust so people give new practices a fair trial before discarding them?
search_terms: pre-read and meeting notes best practices
summary: The post argues that high-performing product teams depend on repetitive, unglamorous meta-work (pre-reads, notes, recaps, learning/decision reviews, and learning backlogs) that is often dismissed as bureaucracy. It’s aimed at product and agile leaders who need a practical way to build team trust and support for these practices by running them as time-bound experiments, expecting an initial dip, and adapting based on feedback.

title: TBM 10/53: Hammers & Nails
questions: How do I recognize when my craft is biasing how I see problems?
What does 'healthy tension' mean in cross-functional teams, and how can it stay healthy?
How can we reduce condescending language and stereotypes between designers, developers, marketers, researchers, and PMs?
What concrete steps can a team take to make roles overlap and contribute more effectively?
search_terms: cross-functional bias between designers and developers
how to challenge my own bias at work
stereotyping between functions in agile teams
summary: This post argues that every discipline in product development—engineering, design, research, marketing, and PM—tends to center its own craft, creating predictable blind spots and sometimes belittling other roles. It’s for founders and product leaders who want healthier cross-functional collaboration by increasing awareness of their own bias and making roles more overlapping rather than siloed.

title: TBM 9/53: Some MVP and Experiment Tips
questions: How do we run many experiments without overwhelming the team or creating too much work in progress?
What makes an MVP “good” for learning rather than a commitment or dependency?
How should we handle safety, blast radius, and communication when experiments can impact others?
When and how should we scale a successful experiment up and out across teams?
search_terms: how to run experiments on a team
summary: This post offers practical guidance for teams running lots of MVPs and experiments, especially when they struggle to keep the work manageable and learning-focused. It emphasizes small, killable bets with clear measurement and pivot/proceed criteria, plus team practices like limiting work in progress, partnering for de-biasing, and communicating blast radius. It’s aimed at product and engineering teams seeking higher experimentation cadence and safer scaling of what works.

title: TBM 8/53: Product Initiative Reviews
questions: How do we review product initiatives when we were building prescriptive features rather than explicitly stating outcomes?
What have we learned recently from our work, not just from our process?
Are we shipping things, and do we know whether they have impact?
How can we run reviews in a vulnerable, non-blame way that invites cross-team learning?
summary: The post argues for running product initiative reviews and learning reviews to determine what happened, what was learned, and whether shipped work had the intended impact. It is aimed at product teams and leaders who want to improve evidence of outcomes—especially when teams are busy or considering organizational changes. The guidance emphasizes vulnerability, balanced assessment, and inviting cross-team participation to avoid blame and “success theater.”

title: TBM 7/53: Learning Backlogs
questions: How should we present multiple workflow options and write the copy that describes them?
Do new workflow options improve campaign efficacy or reinforce anti-patterns?
What should we learn first before arguing about the best research or testing approach?
How do we sequence research questions when we have limited time and many uncertainties?
search_terms: cost of being wrong vs value of being less wrong
summary: The post proposes a “learning backlog” (or “to-learn list”) approach for product teams to manage uncertainty when planning workflow or feature changes in B2B SaaS. It’s aimed at teams doing UX/product discovery who need to triage questions by risk and opportunity, sequence what to learn first, and avoid getting stuck debating research methods instead of clarifying the underlying “why.”

title: TBM 4/53:  Teach by Starting Together
questions: What’s the tradeoff between small-group prework and starting together as a whole team?
How do we ensure junior team members learn discovery and shaping, not just consume outputs?
What alternatives exist when teams can’t start together?
How can rotations or bridge roles preserve learning while keeping schedules manageable?
summary: The post argues that teams should “start together” to ensure everyone learns the messy discovery and shaping process, not just the final artifacts. It contrasts this with a common small-group prework approach that can improve short-term efficiency but silo learning and reduce resiliency. It offers an experiment—using rotations and a bridge role—to spread discovery exposure when full starting together isn’t possible.
